Concept #13 of 52 — Equity Education
Foundry vs Fabless — Why It Matters for ANET
In your portfolio right now, ANET is a fabless chip company. It designs proprietary ASICs (application-specific integrated circuits — chips built for one job, like routing packets at 800G), but outsources all manufacturing to a foundry.
Fabless: Design the chip. Pay a foundry to build it. No factory costs. Margins stay high. ANET gross margin: ~65%. You can scale revenue without scaling capex.
Foundry: Run the factory. TSMC spends $30B+/year just to stay at cutting-edge process nodes. Margins are lower, but the moat is physical — you cannot build a rival fab in two years.
Why this is live risk for ANET: Fabless companies are exposed to foundry allocation risk. If hyperscaler AI chip demand spikes in H2 2026, TSMC prioritizes Apple and NVIDIA — their largest customers. ANET may face production queue delays for its EOS ASIC. This is the sourcing constraint behind the July 25 hard exit deadline. Not a crisis, but a real H2 risk to watch in Q2 earnings language.

New term today — ASIC: Application-Specific Integrated Circuit. A chip engineered to do exactly one thing at maximum efficiency. ANET’s EOS ASIC is optimized purely for ultra-low-latency network switching in AI clusters. It cannot be repurposed. That specialization is ANET’s edge in the hyperscaler market.
